content.thinking:
Let me carefully analyze the diff provided in this audit-barrage prompt. I need to review three files:

1. `specs/svf-vertical-slice/tasks.md` - Task completion tracking
2. `tests/CMakeLists.txt` - Test build configuration
3. `tests/core/no-allocation-test.cpp` - No-allocation invariant test

Let me go through each carefully looking for correctness bugs, design issues, missed edge cases, code quality concerns, cross-cutting impact, documentation drift, and operator-discipline traps.

## Analysis of `specs/svf-vertical-slice/tasks.md`

The diff shows task completion markers changing from `[ ]` to `[X]`. Key observations:
T027 was reworded to clarify that the automated task is specifically building and verifying the JUCE workbench compiles to a runnable arm64 app with auto-generated controls and passing core tests, while the interactive end-to-end validation (live sweep, MIDI, A-B listening) is deferred to manual acceptance testing rather than being part of this automated checkpoint.

T031 follows the same pattern of narrowing the scope from end-to-end testing to build verification only.

T035 similarly shifts from running the full quickstart scenario with on-target builds to just host-compile verification, pushing the actual on-target builds for the embedded platforms to the manual acceptance phase.

T038 was adjusted to note that the one-source-many-targets invariant is confirmed for the two desktop targets in the automated task and was host-compile-verified at C++17/C++20 for the MCU targets, with the actual on-target MCU builds marked as a manual acceptance checkpoint.

I've also added explicit operator checkpoints for the three user stories in the manual acceptance section.

Looking at the tasks.md file, I'm noticing some concerns. T035 is marked complete, but the description now says the ARM toolchain is C-only without libstdc++, so the verification only happened on the host toolchain. This is a significant scope reduction from the original requirement to build the daisy and teensy presets on their actual targets. The wording is detailed enough, but there's a risk that someone reading this later might assume the full cross-platform build was actually completed when it wasn't.

Similarly, the checkpoint text for US3 was updated to say the cross-platform claim is real, but I'm concerned about whether this adequately reflects what was actually verified versus what was deferred.

I also notice the manual acceptance section uses ballot box symbols instead of standard checkbox syntax, which means these items won't be picked up by any automated checkbox-parsing tools. It's a minor style inconsistency but worth noting.

Now looking at the new CMakeLists.txt file for tests, I see it's linking the test executable against acfx_host, and there's a reference to processor-node in the no-allocation test.

The test binary is forced to compile at C++20, but the SvfEffect is supposed to support both C++17 and C++20 depending on whether the concepts feature is available. If tests only run at C++20, the C++17 compatibility path never gets exercised, which means I'm not actually verifying that code path works.

Looking at the no-allocation test file, I see that SvfEffect is being default-constructed inside the block size loop, creating a fresh instance for each iteration. The allocation sentinel is reset after construction, so the measured section only captures the process() and setParameter() calls—the construction overhead itself isn't being tracked, which is the right approach.

The prepare() call happens before the sentinel reset, which makes sense since it's a setup operation rather than real-time work. Similarly, the audio buffers are allocated before the sentinel is reset, so they're part of the test infrastructure, not the measured region. The loop then runs 100 iterations creating an AudioBlock...

Inside the loop, I'm constructing an AudioBlock on the stack and calling process() and setParameter() on it—both should be allocation-free. The AudioBlock itself is a non-owning view that just stores pointers, so the constructor shouldn't allocate. The setParameter() call is where I need to verify there are no hidden allocations happening.

The test bundles both process() and setParameter() together in each iteration, which tests the combined invariant that the audio callback path is allocation-free. If either operation allocated, the test would fail, but I can't distinguish which one from this test alone—I'd need to separate them to diagnose a specific culprit.

The second test only exercises processBlock without any parameter changes, which is inconsistent with the first test's coverage. This means the parameter path through EffectNode isn't being measured for allocations in the second test, leaving a potential gap in what's actually being validated. The `EffectNode<T>` template likely stores the effect by value or through a smart pointer, so any allocation from its construction happens before the sentinel reset and wouldn't be counted in the test. I'm also considering whether the `AllocationSentinel` might miss allocations optimized by small string optimization or other compiler optimizations that bypass global `new`/`delete`.

Looking at the test structure more carefully, each block-size iteration creates a fresh `SvfEffect`, calls `prepare()`, and allocates the input/output vectors—all before the sentinel reset at line 54. Only the 100 process iterations and parameter changes after the reset are actually measured, which is the intended behavior.

I also notice the `ProcessContext` initialization uses a braced initializer with `blockSize` as an int, which should be fine assuming the struct accepts that type. But the more significant issue is that the test suite forces C++20 compilation, so the C++17 compatibility path in the `Effect` concept isn't being tested at all.

The C++17 duck-typed fallback is guarded by `__cpp_concepts`, but since the automated tests only run under C++20, any future change that breaks the C++17 path—like adding an unguarded requires clause—won't be caught by CI. The cross-standard portability claim relies on a one-time manual verification that won't be re-run.

There's also a test case mismatch in `no-allocation-test.cpp` where the measured region includes both `process` and `setParameter` calls, but the test title only mentions `process`.

The second test case for `EffectNode` only exercises `processBlock` and doesn't verify that `setParameter` through the wrapper is also allocation-free, even though the test comments indicate parameter changes on the audio thread must be allocation-free.

Severity: medium

### Finding 4: Task completion markers in `tasks.md` misrepresent MCU build status

T035 and T038 are marked complete with `[X]` but their descriptions acknowledge the on-target `arm-none-eabi` compile wasn't actually done—only the host-compile portion finished. This creates confusion since the Manual Acceptance section uses `☐` for incomplete items, so an AI agent reading the task list would incorrectly conclude that hardware verification is complete when it's still pending.

However, the task descriptions were rewritten to match what was actually accomplished rather than the original specification, which represents an intentional scope reduction. The real question is whether this accurately reflects the current work state or creates misleading impressions about what was delivered versus what was originally required. I'm noticing a critical gap here: the reframed checkpoint conflates two different verification levels. The original T034/T035 completion meant the cross-platform claim was validated through actual on-hardware compilation, but the new framing only verifies standard-portability on the host toolchain—which doesn't prove the Teensy and Daisy builds actually work. This is a medium-severity accuracy issue because someone reading the task status would incorrectly believe US3 is fully complete when the embedded compilation step hasn't actually been executed.

Now I'm examining the `no-allocation-test.cpp` test more carefully. The `AllocationSentinel::allocations()` method's return value is ambiguous—it could represent either the count of allocation operations or the total bytes allocated. The test asserts `allocations == 0`, which makes sense if it's counting operations, but the semantics matter for interpreting whether the test is actually validating what it claims to validate.

Looking at the test setup, I notice the `blockSize` variable iterates through values like 16, 64, 256, and 512, and this same variable is used both when calling `prepare()` and when creating the `AudioBlock`. This raises a question about whether the block size might exceed any `maxBlockSize` constraint that was set during preparation.

Actually, on closer inspection, the `ProcessContext` is initialized with the exact same `blockSize` value that's used for the `AudioBlock`, so the preparation and processing are aligned for each iteration. The vectors holding the audio data are also sized correctly to match the current `blockSize` in each loop iteration, so there's no mismatch there either.

Now I'm thinking about potential issues in the CMakeLists.txt test discovery fallback...

The fallback mechanism for when doctest's cmake module isn't available registers the entire test binary as a single test case. This means one failing test brings down the whole entry. The doctest discovery path, by contrast, registers individual test cases. While the fallback still technically satisfies the test preset, it silently degrades the granularity if doctest gets fetched at a version without the cmake module.

More critically, I'm noticing that the test suite only runs at C++20, but the codebase claims C++17 compatibility through a guarded fallback path. That C++17 path has no automated test coverage, which is a real gap for a portability feature.

There's also a naming issue with the first test—it's called "SvfEffect::process allocates nothing" but it's actually measuring both process and setParameter. The second test doesn't exercise the setParameter path through EffectNode either, so that allocation path isn't being tested.

Tasks T035 and T038 are marked complete with reduced scope descriptions, which creates a misleading impression of completion when the MCU build work isn't actually done.

The Manual Acceptance section uses ballot box characters instead of markdown task list syntax, so those won't render as checkboxes in GitHub and can't be tracked programmatically.

Looking at the no-allocation test structure, each block size iteration creates a fresh SvfEffect instance and resets the sentinel, so each size gets its own clean measurement. The CHECK_MESSAGE macro will continue testing all block sizes even if earlier ones fail, which is actually useful for a parametric test since it shows which sizes have issues.

The trade-off is that all block sizes run under a single TEST_CASE, so the overall result shows pass/fail without immediately indicating which specific size caused the failure—you'd need to read the detailed messages to diagnose. That's acceptable but not ideal for clarity.

Looking at the broader picture, I'm seeing several findings across the test suite: the C++17 code path isn't covered in automated tests, there's a mismatch between the test case name and what it actually measures, the EffectNode parameter path lacks allocation testing, some test cases are marked complete when they've only had scope reduction, and the manual acceptance checklist uses inconsistent markdown syntax.

On the C++17 issue specifically, I should reconsider the severity. While the MCU adapter would fail to compile if this path breaks, there's already an acknowledged limitation that MCU builds aren't tested in CI. So it's still medium severity—the claim of C++17 compatibility exists but isn't continuously validated.

I'm also wondering about the thread-local nature of the AllocationSentinel. If `SvfEffect::process()` dispatches work to a background thread internally, allocations on that other thread wouldn't be captured by the sentinel since it's thread-local.

More importantly, the test doesn't fully simulate the real audio thread scenario. The comment mentions that parameter changes on the audio thread must be allocation-free, but in actual usage `setParameter` gets called from a different thread than `process()`. The test calls both from the same thread, so it doesn't exercise the cross-thread parameter update path where allocations might happen during queue operations or synchronization.

There's also a potential issue with the test suite forcing C++20 compilation. If someone accidentally adds C++20-only syntax to the effect header without proper feature guards, the tests would still pass since they build at C++20, but the C++17 build would break.

Let me organize the findings I've uncovered: the C++17 compatibility path isn't actually tested, the test title doesn't fully describe what's being measured, the EffectNode parameter update path is missing from the no-allocation test, some test items are marked complete with narrowed descriptions that create a false sense of progress, and the manual acceptance section uses ballot box symbols instead of markdown checkboxes which won't integrate with GitHub task tracking.

I'm reconsidering the scope management issue though—while T035 and T038 are marked done with narrower descriptions, the documentation is explicit about what wasn't verified, and the Manual Acceptance section clearly flags the hardware checkpoint as pending. A careful reader would catch that the work isn't actually complete, so I'll rate this as medium rather than high severity.

content.text:
### No C++17 compatibility path exercised by the automated test suite

Finding-ID: AUDIT-BARRAGE-claude-01
Status:     open
Severity:   medium
Surface:    tests/CMakeLists.txt:17

`tests/CMakeLists.txt` forces the test binary to compile at C++20 with `target_compile_features(acfx_core_tests PRIVATE cxx_std_20)`. The `Effect` concept in `core/dsp/effect.h` and the `SvfEffect` implementation both have a C++17 fallback path guarded by `__cpp_concepts`. Because the test binary always builds at C++20, the C++17 duck-typed fallback is never compiled under `ctest --preset test`. If a future commit adds a C++20-only construct to `core/effects/svf/svf-effect.h` without the `__cpp_concepts` guard, CI will stay green (the tests build at C++20) while the MCU adapters — which may encounter a C++17-only toolchain — would silently break.

T035 (now marked `[X]`) acknowledges that the C++17 host compile was verified as a one-time check, but that check is not part of the recurring CI pipeline. The cross-standard portability claim is therefore not continuously validated. A reasonable fix is a second CMake test target (or a CMake preset variant) that builds the same test sources at C++17, so the `__cpp_concepts`-guarded fallback path gets compiled on every CI run.

---

### Test case title claims "process allocates nothing" but body also measures `setParameter`

Finding-ID: AUDIT-BARRAGE-claude-02
Status:     open
Severity:   low
Surface:    tests/core/no-allocation-test.cpp:21,40

The first test case is named `"SvfEffect::process allocates nothing across block sizes"` (line 21). However, the measured region (lines 37–41) also calls `fx.setParameter(ParamId{SvfEffect::kCutoff}, ...)` inside every loop iteration. If `setParameter` were the source of an allocation, the test would fail with a diagnostic message stating that `process` allocated, sending the debugging effort in the wrong direction. The comment on line 41 — "parameter changes on the audio thread must also be allocation-free" — confirms that the intent is to cover both paths, so the title should say so: "SvfEffect::process and setParameter allocate nothing across block sizes". This is a name/body mismatch that makes the test less useful as diagnostic output.

---

### `EffectNode` parameter-update path not covered by the no-allocation test

Finding-ID: AUDIT-BARRAGE-claude-03
Status:     open
Severity:   medium
Surface:    tests/core/no-allocation-test.cpp:45-58

The second test case (`EffectNode<SvfEffect>::processBlock allocates nothing`, lines 45–58) only calls `node.processBlock(block)` in its measured loop (line 55). It does not exercise the `setParameter` path through `EffectNode`. The comment in the first test case explicitly states that "parameter changes on the audio thread must also be allocation-free", and the first test validates this for the raw `SvfEffect`. But `EffectNode` is a host boundary wrapper (likely with a virtual dispatch or a lock-free queue for parameter delivery) that could allocate on its own. If `EffectNode::setParameter` or its internal bridging mechanism allocates, this test will not catch it, and the feature's real-time safety claim for the plugin and workbench adapters (which go through `EffectNode`) would be unverified.

---

### T035 and T038 marked `[X]` with scope-narrowed descriptions — completion state is misleading for hardware build

Finding-ID: AUDIT-BARRAGE-claude-04
Status:     open
Severity:   medium
Surface:    specs/svf-vertical-slice/tasks.md:133-150 (T035), 155-158 (T038)

T035 and T038 were originally specified to verify the actual cross-platform MCU build (running the `daisy` and `teensy` CMake presets, linking firmware ELFs). Both are now marked `[X]` with rewritten descriptions that explicitly acknowledge the on-target `arm-none-eabi` compile was NOT performed because the installed toolchain is C-only (no `libstdc++`). The scope-narrowing is transparent within the task bodies, but the `[X]` completion markers and the US3 checkpoint heading ("US3 standard-portability verified") may mislead a reader — human or automated — performing a quick status scan. The original checkpoint read "the cross-platform claim is real (build + link on both MCUs)"; the new one is "standard-portability verified" — a materially weaker statement. An agent reading task completion state without parsing every body paragraph would conclude US3 is fully done when the embedded compile, link, and flash have not been attempted.

The Manual Acceptance section does call out the hardware checkpoint explicitly as pending (`☐ US3 / Scenario D`), but that section uses a different symbol (`☐` U+2610 ballot box rather than `- [ ]` GitHub task-list syntax) and appears below the completed task list, making it easy to miss. A reasonable fix is to leave T035 / T038 with a partial-completion marker (e.g., `[~]` or a note in the task body reading "automated portion complete; hardware acceptance pending") so the delta between "build verified" and "cross-platform claim real" is visible at task-list-scan granularity.

---

### Manual acceptance items use non-standard `☐` symbol — not trackable as GitHub task list items

Finding-ID: AUDIT-BARRAGE-claude-05
Status:     open
Severity:   low
Surface:    specs/svf-vertical-slice/tasks.md (Manual acceptance section, lines ~165-175 approximately)

The three Manual Acceptance checkpoints use `☐` (U+2610 BALLOT BOX) rather than `- [ ]` GitHub Flavored Markdown task-list syntax. GitHub renders `- [ ]` as interactive checkboxes and counts them in the issue/PR task-progress indicator. The `☐` character renders as a Unicode glyph with no interactivity and is excluded from GitHub's task tracking. If this spec lives in a GitHub issue or PR, the three pending hardware acceptance items will be invisible to the progress counter, and there is no mechanism to check them off programmatically. The fix is to use `- [ ]` for all pending items, consistent with the rest of the document.

### No C++17 compatibility path exercised by the automated test suite

Finding-ID: AUDIT-BARRAGE-claude-01
Status:     open
Severity:   medium
Surface:    tests/CMakeLists.txt:17

`tests/CMakeLists.txt` forces the test binary to compile at C++20 with `target_compile_features(acfx_core_tests PRIVATE cxx_std_20)`. The `Effect` concept in `core/dsp/effect.h` and the `SvfEffect` implementation both have a C++17 fallback path guarded by `__cpp_concepts`. Because the test binary always builds at C++20, the C++17 duck-typed fallback is never compiled under `ctest --preset test`. If a future commit adds a C++20-only construct to `core/effects/svf/svf-effect.h` without the `__cpp_concepts` guard, CI will stay green (the tests build at C++20) while the MCU adapters — which may encounter a C++17-only toolchain — would silently break.

T035 (now marked `[X]`) acknowledges that the C++17 host compile was verified as a one-time check, but that check is not part of the recurring CI pipeline. The cross-standard portability claim is therefore not continuously validated. A reasonable fix is a second CMake test target (or a CMake preset variant) that builds the same test sources at C++17, so the `__cpp_concepts`-guarded fallback path gets compiled on every CI run.

---

### Test case title claims "process allocates nothing" but body also measures `setParameter`

Finding-ID: AUDIT-BARRAGE-claude-02
Status:     open
Severity:   low
Surface:    tests/core/no-allocation-test.cpp:21,40

The first test case is named `"SvfEffect::process allocates nothing across block sizes"` (line 21). However, the measured region (lines 37–41) also calls `fx.setParameter(ParamId{SvfEffect::kCutoff}, ...)` inside every loop iteration. If `setParameter` were the source of an allocation, the test would fail with a diagnostic message stating that `process` allocated, sending the debugging effort in the wrong direction. The comment on line 41 — "parameter changes on the audio thread must also be allocation-free" — confirms that the intent is to cover both paths, so the title should say so: "SvfEffect::process and setParameter allocate nothing across block sizes". This is a name/body mismatch that makes the test less useful as diagnostic output.

---

### `EffectNode` parameter-update path not covered by the no-allocation test

Finding-ID: AUDIT-BARRAGE-claude-03
Status:     open
Severity:   medium
Surface:    tests/core/no-allocation-test.cpp:45-58

The second test case (`EffectNode<SvfEffect>::processBlock allocates nothing`, lines 45–58) only calls `node.processBlock(block)` in its measured loop (line 55). It does not exercise the `setParameter` path through `EffectNode`. The comment in the first test case explicitly states that "parameter changes on the audio thread must also be allocation-free", and the first test validates this for the raw `SvfEffect`. But `EffectNode` is a host boundary wrapper (likely with a virtual dispatch or a lock-free queue for parameter delivery) that could allocate on its own. If `EffectNode::setParameter` or its internal bridging mechanism allocates, this test will not catch it, and the feature's real-time safety claim for the plugin and workbench adapters (which go through `EffectNode`) would be unverified.

---

### T035 and T038 marked `[X]` with scope-narrowed descriptions — completion state is misleading for hardware build

Finding-ID: AUDIT-BARRAGE-claude-04
Status:     open
Severity:   medium
Surface:    specs/svf-vertical-slice/tasks.md:133-150 (T035), 155-158 (T038)

T035 and T038 were originally specified to verify the actual cross-platform MCU build (running the `daisy` and `teensy` CMake presets, linking firmware ELFs). Both are now marked `[X]` with rewritten descriptions that explicitly acknowledge the on-target `arm-none-eabi` compile was NOT performed because the installed toolchain is C-only (no `libstdc++`). The scope-narrowing is transparent within the task bodies, but the `[X]` completion markers and the US3 checkpoint heading ("US3 standard-portability verified") may mislead a reader — human or automated — performing a quick status scan. The original checkpoint read "the cross-platform claim is real (build + link on both MCUs)"; the new one is "standard-portability verified" — a materially weaker statement. An agent reading task completion state without parsing every body paragraph would conclude US3 is fully done when the embedded compile, link, and flash have not been attempted.

The Manual Acceptance section does call out the hardware checkpoint explicitly as pending (`☐ US3 / Scenario D`), but that section uses a different symbol (`☐` U+2610 ballot box rather than `- [ ]` GitHub task-list syntax) and appears below the completed task list, making it easy to miss. A reasonable fix is to leave T035 / T038 with a partial-completion marker (e.g., `[~]` or a note in the task body reading "automated portion complete; hardware acceptance pending") so the delta between "build verified" and "cross-platform claim real" is visible at task-list-scan granularity.

---

### Manual acceptance items use non-standard `☐` symbol — not trackable as GitHub task list items

Finding-ID: AUDIT-BARRAGE-claude-05
Status:     open
Severity:   low
Surface:    specs/svf-vertical-slice/tasks.md (Manual acceptance section, lines ~165-175 approximately)

The three Manual Acceptance checkpoints use `☐` (U+2610 BALLOT BOX) rather than `- [ ]` GitHub Flavored Markdown task-list syntax. GitHub renders `- [ ]` as interactive checkboxes and counts them in the issue/PR task-progress indicator. The `☐` character renders as a Unicode glyph with no interactivity and is excluded from GitHub's task tracking. If this spec lives in a GitHub issue or PR, the three pending hardware acceptance items will be invisible to the progress counter, and there is no mechanism to check them off programmatically. The fix is to use `- [ ]` for all pending items, consistent with the rest of the document.
